content.full_text:
A-01-

See Advice Ltr. No. 159

SUPERSEDED

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


May 11, 2001


David Bauer
400 Capitol Mall, Ste. 1560
Sacramento, CA 95814


Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-01-044

Dear Mr. Bauer:

     You have requested advice on behalf of State Senator Maurice Johannessen
concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ By
telephone on February 27, 2001, you stated that you are authorized to request this advice
on behalf of Senator Johannessen.

QUESTION

     What contribution limits are applicable to a controlled committee established by
Senator Johannessen for a 1993 special election for purposes of raising funds to pay debts
in connection with that election?

CONCLUSION

     Proposition 34, which was passed by the voters in the November 2000 General
Election, repealed the special election contribution limits previously enacted as part of
Proposition 73 and established new limits for candidates for elective state office. The
applicability of Proposition 34's contribution limits to debt retirement for elections held
prior to January 1, 2001, is one of many issues the Commission will address later this

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

David Bauer
Our File No. A-01-044
Page 2

year. In the meantime, we recommend that contributions to retire debt from Senator
Johannessen's 1993 special election continue be raised under the special election limits
established by Proposition 73.

FACTS

Maurice Johannessen is an incumbent state senator who is serving his last term in
the Senate due to term limits. In addition to the committee formed for his reelection in
1998, he has a controlled committee that was formed in 1993 to support his candidacy in
a special election for the seat he currently holds. The special election committee has debt
remaining from the special election.

ANALYSIS

Contributions to candidates for state office were made subject to monetary limits
after passage of Proposition 73 in 1988. The contribution limits of Proposition 73 were
applied separately during a special election cycle and a special runoff election cycle,² as
follows:

Contributor                                    Contribution Limit (per cycle)

Person                                         $1,000
Political Committee                            $2,500
Broad Based Political Committee               $5,000
Political Party                                $5,000

Regulation 18535 allowed special election candidates to continue raising funds
after the special election to pay debt, up to the applicable limits per election.³

Section 85305 was repealed by the passage of Proposition 34 in November 2000
and new contribution limits went into effect January 1, 2001. Candidates for state
legislative offices are now subject to the following contribution limits set out in sections
85301 and 85302 in connection with all elections, including special and special runoff
elections:


² Election cycles were defined in section 85305(b). "Special election cycle" means the day on which an
elective office becomes vacant until the day of the special election. "Special runoff election cycle" means
the day after the special election until the day of the special runoff election. Identical language appears in
section 85204.5, as added by Proposition 34.

³ Regulation 18535 was repealed by the Commission on March 9, 2001.

David Bauer
Our File No. A-01-044
Page 3

Contributor                          Contribution Limit (per election)

Person⁴                              $3,000
Small Contributor Committee⁵         $6,000
Political Party                      No limit

Post-election fundraising is now governed by new section 85316, which states:

"A contribution for an election may be accepted by a candidate
for elective state office after the date of the election only to the
extent that the contribution does not exceed net debts outstanding
from the election, and the contribution does not otherwise exceed
the applicable contribution limit for that election."

Section 85316 is silent as to which contribution limits should be applied to debts
from a special election held prior to January 1, 2001. Our Commission will be
considering this and other issues during the coming year. Until that time, we recommend
that Senator Johannessen abide by the contribution limits in place during 1993 for
purposes of raising funds to pay debt. In other words, the Senator may accept
contributions to pay debt so long as the aggregate amount received from any one
contributor does not exceed the applicable Proposition 73 limit.

If you have questions concerning his letter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By:    Carla Wardlow
       Division Chief
       Technical Assistance Division


⁴ "Person" means an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust,
company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and any other organization or
group of persons acting in concert. (Section 82047.)

⁵ A "small contributor committee" is a committee that has been in existence for six months, receives
contributions from 100 or more persons, has not received more than $200 per calendar year from any single
contributor, and makes contributions to five or more candidates. (Section 85203; see also Regulation
18503.)
